item.title: The __set_name__ method for descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Descriptors generally have to interact with attributes of the managed object, and this is done by inspecting __dict__ on that object (or calling getattr/setattr, but the problem is the same), and finding the key under the specific name.

For this reaôÙson, the deôÙscripôÙtor will have to know the name of the key to look for, which is reôÙlatôÙed to the name of the atôÙtribute is manôÙagôÙing.

On preôÙviôÙous verôÙsions of Python this had to be done exôÙplicôÙitôÙly. If we wantôÙed to work around it, there were some more adôÙvanced ways to do so. LuckôÙiôÙly, afôÙter PEP-487 (added in Python 3.6), there are some enôÙhanceôÙments reôÙgardôÙing class creôÙation, which alôÙso afôÙfects deôÙscripôÙtors.

Let's reôÙview the probôÙlem, the preôÙviôÙous apôÙproachôÙes to tackôÙle it, and the modôÙern way of solvôÙing it.

Read moreãÎ (4 min remaining to read)

item.title: Descriptors & Decorators
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
DeôÙscripôÙtors are an amazôÙing tool to have in our toolôÙbox, as they come in handy in many opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙties.

ProbôÙaôÙbly the best thing about deôÙscripôÙtors, is that they can imôÙprove othôÙer soôÙluôÙtionôÙs. Let's see how we can write betôÙter decôÙoôÙraôÙtors, by usôÙing deôÙscripôÙtors.

Read moreãÎ (5 min remaining to read)

item.title: __wrapped__ in Python decorators
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This is anôÙothôÙer of the new inôÙterôÙestôÙing things that Python 3 has. Time ago, it was someôÙhow tricky to work with decôÙoôÙratôÙed funcôÙtionôÙs, beôÙcause the decôÙoôÙraôÙtor reôÙplaced the origôÙiôÙnal obôÙjecôÙt, and its beôÙhavôÙiour beôÙcame hard to reach.

Read moreãÎ (2 min remaining to read)
